query:
UPDATE blog_posts SET content = 'We have helped over 40 first-time visitors plan their China trips across Facebook and Reddit travel groups. The same questions keep coming up. Here are honest answers, drawn from real conversations with real travelers. We will keep updating this post as new common themes emerge.

## How many nights should I spend in each city?

Beijing deserves 4 to 5 nights minimum. Shanghai is more manageable in size but rewards slowing down with 3 to 4 nights. Xi''an needs 3 nights because the Terracotta Warriors are a full day experience. Chengdu deserves 3 nights for pandas, Sichuan food, and Leshan Giant Buddha. Chongqing is dramatic and worth 2 to 3 nights. Zhangjiajie needs 3 nights.

The travelers who enjoy China most are the ones who do fewer cities better, not more cities faster. The Golden Triangle (Beijing, Shanghai, Xi''an) comes up constantly because it works—three iconic cities, logical routing, well-connected by high-speed train. For 10 to 14 days it is hard to beat. If you have under 10 days, the Golden Triangle gets squeezed. If you have 3 weeks or more, do the Golden Triangle and add a nature destination like Zhangjiajie, or a slower city like Chengdu or Chongqing.

## What about transportation between cities?

High-speed trains are almost always the right call over domestic flights once you factor in airport time. Beijing to Xi''an overnight sleeper is a classic. Xi''an to Shanghai in 6 hours on a day train is comfortable and scenic. Keep Xi''an, especially for a first trip. The Terracotta Warriors are genuinely one of the most impressive things you will see anywhere in the world.

## What about food and dietary needs?

The Muslim Quarter has some of the best street food in China. For food, China is actually great for young children. Plain steamed rice, congee, steamed egg, noodles, and dumplings are mild, soft, and available everywhere. Chengdu is the one city to watch, as the default cooking is spicy. Ask explicitly for bu yao la (no spice).

## When should I go? What about holidays?

China has several major holidays where domestic travel spikes significantly. Chinese New Year is the biggest migration event in the world. Trains and flights book out weeks in advance. Labour Day Golden Week (May 1 to 5) is a significant travel peak. Popular sites get crowded and trains fill up fast. National Day Golden Week (October 1 to 7) is the busiest travel period after Chinese New Year. Trains sell out weeks in advance and major tourist sites reach genuinely overwhelming crowd levels.

Qingming Festival (early April, date varies) is a 3-day holiday that spikes domestic travel noticeably. Less extreme than the others but worth knowing about, especially for train bookings.

If your trip overlaps with any of these, spend the holiday days in a large city rather than a scenic mountain area. Chongqing during Golden Week is far more manageable than Zhangjiajie. Book trains as early as possible, ideally the moment bookings open (usually 15 days in advance). Build buffer days into your itinerary so one delayed train doesn''t collapse the whole plan.

## We''re going in June. Anything we should know about the weather?

More than most people expect. Weather is the thing first-timers research last and should research first.

June through August is hot and humid across most of eastern China. Shanghai, Beijing, and Chongqing can all exceed 35°C. Not unpleasant if you''re prepared, but exhausting if you''re not. Front-load outdoor sightseeing in the mornings.

Zhangjiajie in spring and early summer is often misty and rainy. This is not necessarily bad. The mountains in mist are genuinely spectacular. But for clear blue sky views, October is more reliable.

Yunnan (Kunming, Dali, Lijiang) is the exception to the summer heat rule. Kunming sits at altitude and rarely exceeds 25°C even in June, earning its nickname "Spring City." A good option if heat is a real concern.

October is widely considered the best month for China travel. Cooler temperatures, clearer skies, good visibility for mountains and landscapes. Just avoid the first week because of National Day Golden Week.

May is generally excellent but check your dates carefully. May 1 to 5 is Labour Day Golden Week and catches a lot of first-timers off guard.

## We''re traveling with a toddler. Is this manageable?

More manageable than most people expect, with a few honest caveats.

Shanghai and Beijing are the most stroller-friendly cities. Wide pavements, generally good metro accessibility, and most major tourist sites have paved paths. Chongqing is the trickiest because the city is extremely hilly and vertical, with a lot of stairs around the old city. Expect to fold and carry more there.

Timing matters too. Late October and November are excellent for family trips. The heat is gone, crowds are thinner, and the weather across Shanghai and Beijing is genuinely pleasant.

One practical note: a lightweight travel stroller you can carry for short stretches is better than a heavy pram. Bring a carrier as backup for uneven terrain.

## Everyone keeps telling me to relax my itinerary. How do I actually do that?

Build in at least one full free day per week and don''t schedule it.

The travelers who come back most satisfied are rarely the ones who ticked off the most cities. They''re the ones who spent an unplanned afternoon wandering the French Concession, stumbled into Fuxing Park and watched locals do ballroom dancing, or found a hole-in-the-wall shengjianbao spot that they still think about years later.

The itinerary is a framework, not a contract.

## I''ve done the Bund. What else is there in Shanghai?

More than most people give it credit for.

**Fuxing Park on a weekend morning** - Ballroom dancing, tai chi, card games. Zero tourists. Pure local Shanghai.

**Wukang Road** - Walk it slowly. The architecture rewards lingering and there are good independent cafes tucked into the old buildings.

**Shengjianbao from a street spot, not a restaurant** - The hole-in-the-wall version will ruin the fancy version for you permanently.

**Suzhou or Zhujiajiao as a day trip** - Classical gardens and canals, under an hour from Shanghai by bullet train. A completely different pace.

**West Bund Art Center or Rockbund Art Museum** - If contemporary art is your thing. World class and rarely crowded.

If you want to go deeper on what everyday Shanghai life actually looks like, we run +1 Chopsticks (plus1chopsticks.com), a home dining experience with our local English-speaking family. Guests come for a home-cooked Shanghai meal and usually end up staying far longer than planned. It''s one of the few ways to see past the tourist layer of the city without having to work hard to find it.

Have a question about your China itinerary? Subscribe to our newsletter by leaving your email at plus1chopsticks.com, or get in touch directly at plusonechopsticks@gmail.com

Last updated: March 2026', excerpt = 'We have helped over 40 first-time visitors plan their China trips. Here are honest answers to the questions that keep coming up, drawn from real conversations with real travelers. We will keep updating this post as new common themes emerge.' WHERE slug = 'planning-first-china-trip-itinerary-questions'
